courses.subject: ARTS
courses.title: Generative AI and Creative Expressions

meetings.instructor:
Dr. ZHOU Mengyuan, 
Professor Collier Desha NOGUES

Dr. ZHOU Mengyuan, 
Professor Collier Desha NOGUES

Dr. ZHOU Mengyuan, 
Professor Collier Desha NOGUES

courses.description: We know that Generative AI can, well, generate. But is “generating” the same thing as “creating”? Is creative expression a uniquely human capacity, or can we share it with machines? This course seeks potential answers in four stages. We begin by introducing definitions of “creativity” from a variety of disciplines, enabling students to develop a flexible working understanding of the term. The course then turns to the mathematical back end of Generative AI, equipping students with the knowledge to evaluate the degree to which GenAI “thinks” or “creates” like humans do. In the course’s third stage, we will engage with ethical questions that contextualize our inquiry, focusing on GenAI’s training data bias, environmental consequences, copyright issues, and user accessibility and opportunity disparities. Finally, we will explore the growing body of critical literature about how well Generative AI “creates” in different media and forms, including fiction, poetry, photography, painting, music, and film. Is GenAI better at some creative disciplines than others, and if so, what can that tell us about the nature of creativity and creative expression? Throughout, the course will emphasize practice-based learning, offering multiple chances for students to co-create with GenAI models, and to develop their own critical rubrics to evaluate GenAI and human creative expression.
courses.learning_outcomes:
1. Critical Description of AI Technologies: Students will be able to describe the recent history of generative AI technologies and their implications for creative practices, including their impact on authorship, originality, and artistic expression.
2. Interdisciplinary Understanding: Students will demonstrate an understanding of the interdisciplinary nature of generative AI, incorporating perspectives from literature, philosophy, art, and technology to explore how these fields intersect.
3. Creative Application of AI Tools: Students will gain hands-on experience using generative AI tools to create original works in various mediums (e.g., text, visual art, music) while understanding the underlying algorithms and methodologies.
4. Ethical Considerations: Students will evaluate the ethical considerations surrounding the use of generative AI in creative fields, including issues of bias, intellectual property, environmental burden, unequal access, and the potential for misuse.
courses.course_syllabus:
Week 1: Introduction to Generative AI
1. 1 The Making of AI: a brief history of how our digital world came to be
1. 2 Overview of generative AI: definitions and applications

Week 2: Foundations of Creativity I: Human Creativity
2. 1 Comparative exploration of definitions of creativity in arts and humanities: literature, music, visual art, and creative industries (architecture, fashion, graphic design, etc.)
2. 2 Discussion of individual vs. collaborative creative work

Week 3: Foundations of Creativity II: Human vs. Machine Creativity
3. 1 Definitions of creativity in AI context
3. 2 Historical context of AI in creative fields

First tutorial: Studio experiment #1

Week 4: Understanding Generative Models I: LLMs
4. 1 Introduction to key building blocks of generative models (embedding space, parameters, pre-training and fine-tuning, prompting)
4. 2 Basics of how AI generates creative works

Second tutorial: Studio experiment #2

First journal entry due

Week 5: Understanding Generative Models II: multimodal models (e.g. text-to-image/video/sound)
5. 1 Introduction to key generative multimodal models and how they work (embedding, alignment)
5. 2 What do multimodal models "know"?

Third tutorial: Studio experiment #3

Week 6: Ethical Implications of AI in Creativity I
6. 1 copyright and data extraction
6. 2 bias and functionality fallacies

Fourth tutorial: Studio experiment #4

Second journal entry due

Week 7: Ethical Implications of AI in Creativity II
7. 1 environmental burden
7. 2 resource allocation gaps and access disparities

Fifth tutorial: Studio experiment #5

Week 8: AI in Creative Expressions Fundamentals Review
8. 1 Interactive review of human vs. machine creativity; how generative models work; and AI ethics, spotlighting challenging material and emphasizing a firm grasp of AI fundamentals
8. 2 Reflective discussion about course outcomes so far

Sixth tutorial: continuation of experiments 1-4 (per individual student choice)

Week 9: AI in Literature
9. 1 Case studies of AI-generated texts (poetry, prose) and their public reception
9. 2 Analysis of authorship and originality in AI-generated literature

Seventh tutorial: Studio experiment #6

Third journal entry due

Week 10: AI in Visual Arts
10. 1 Exploration of AI-generated visual art (painting, photography, film)
10. 2 Discussion of notable AI artists and artworks and their public reception

Eighth tutorial: Studio experiment #7

Week 11: Music and Sound Creation with AI
11. 1 Examination of AI-generated music compositions and their public reception
11. 2 Tools and software for AI music generation

Ninth tutorial: Studio experiment #8

Fourth journal entry due

Week 12: AI and Creative Industries
12. 1 Impact of generative AI on cultural narratives and production in the fields of architecture, fashion, and graphic design

Tenth tutorial: planning for your final project

Week 13: The Future
13. 1 Where the field is going: developments in multi-agent systems and their potential for sophisticated generative AI
13. 2 Final Project Workshop and Course Reflection: students present final projects in peer groups

Eleventh tutorial: initial peer feedback on final project plans and reflective discussion on learning outcomes
courses.feedback_evaluation:
Students may raise questions or offer comments during class sessions, as well as via email or during individual office hours.

At the end of the course, students will be asked to complete Course Evaluations for both the lectures and tutorials for the course. Both the instructors and the Faculty value this information highly, as it allows us to understand what we are doing well and how we can improve. We use your feedback to revise our teaching methods and course content.
courses.required_readings: Bender, et al., “On the Dangers of Stochastic Parrots,” Association for Computing Machinery FaccT, 2021.
courses.recommended_readings:
Inioluwa Deborah Raji, Elizabeth Kumar, Aaron Horowitz, Andrew Selbst, “The Fallacy of AI Functionality.” Association for Computing Machinery FaccT, 2022.

Vaswan, Ashish, Noam Shazeer, Niki Parmar, Jakob Uszkoreit, Llion Jones, Aidan N. Gomez, Łukasz Kaiser, et al. 2017. “Attention Is All You Need.” In 31st Conference on Neural Information Processing Systems, edited by Ulrike von Luxburg, Isabelle Guyon, Samy Bengio, Hanna Wallach, and Rob Fergus, 6000-6010. New York: Curran Associates.

OpenAI, Josh Achiam, Steven Adler, Sandhini Agarwal, Lama Ahmad, Ilge Akkaya, Florencia Leoni Aleman, et al. 2023. “GPT-4 Technical Report.” Preprint, submitted in 2023. https://arxiv.org/abs/2303.08774.

Nature Editorial. 2024. “Why Scientists Trust AI Too Much — And What To Do About It.” Nature 627.

Publications on creativity from creative disciplines:

Baron, Naomi. Who Wrote This? How AI and the Lure of Efficiency Threaten Human Writing. Stanford UP, 2023.

Chiang, Ted. 2023. “ChatGPT Is a Blurry JPEG of the Web.” The New Yorker, February 9, 2023.

D’Erasmo, Stacy. The Long Run: A Creative Inquiry. Graywolf, 2024.

Moss, Adam. The Work of Art: How Something Comes from Nothing. Penguin, 2024.

Andrew Sullivan, “I used to be a human being,” New Yorker, September 2016.
